elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce an extension of the Difference of Convex Algorithm (DCA) in the form of a block coordinate approach for problems with separable structure.
elements.sent-2411-002: For $n$ coordinate-blocks and $k$ iterations, our main result proves a non-asymptotic convergence rate of $O(n/k)$ for the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-003: Furthermore, leveraging the connection between DCA and Expectation Maximization (EM), we propose a block coordinate EM algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-004: In nonconvex optimization, many problems find their abstraction through the lens of Difference of Convex (DC) programming, which involves minimizing an objective function represented as the difference of convex functions.
elements.sent-2411-005: This paper focuses on the following DC programming template: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:main} \min_{\bm{x}\in \mathcal{M}} ~~ \phi(\bm{x}) := f(\bm{x}) + g(\bm{x}) - h(\bm{x}),\end{equation} where $f$, $g$, and $h$ are lower semi-continuous convex functions defined on the closed convex set $\mathcal{M \subseteq \mathbb{R ^m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: We assume that the function $f$ is smooth, and that the function $g$ and the set $\mathcal{M}$ exhibit a block-separable structure in relation to the coordinates of $\bm{x}$ (see \Cref{sec:BC-DC ).}
elements.sent-2411-007: We do not impose the smoothness assumption on the terms $g$ and $h$.
elements.sent-2411-008: A large and popular class of algorithms for solving problem~\eqref{eqn:main , introduced by \citet{TAO1986249 , comprises instances of the DCA method.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: These algorithms rely on the simple yet effective idea of approximating $\phi(\bm{x )$ with the convex surrogate function obtained by using the first-order Taylor approximation of the concave component $-h(\bm{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Starting from a feasible initial point $\bm{x ^0 \in \mathcal{M}$, DCA solves a sequence of convex optimization subproblems defined as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:main-surrogate} \min_{\bm{x}\in \mathcal{M}} ~~ \hat{\phi}(\bm{x},\bm{x}^k) := f(\bm{x}) + g(\bm{x}) - h(\bm{x}^k) - \ip{\nabla h(\bm{x}^k)}{\bm{x} - \bm{x}^k},\end{equation} where $\bm{x ^k$ represents the current estimate.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:main-surrogate} \min_{x\in M} ~~ \hat{\phi}(x,x^k) := f(x) + g(x) - h(x^k) - \ip{\nabla h(x^k)}{x - x^k},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-011: Observe that function $\hat{\phi}$ is a global upper bound on $\phi$ since $h$ is convex; as a result, DCA can be viewed as a variant of the Majorization–Minimization (MM) algorithm \citep{lange2016mm .}

elements.sent-2411-013: The general template of DCA does not specify how, or to what degree of precision, we should solve these subproblems, and various instances can be derived by employing different techniques for their resolution.
elements.sent-2411-014: Motivated by its strong empirical performance, wide applicability, simplicity, and its interpretablity, DCA has been extensively studied.
elements.sent-2411-015: With the recent focus in machine learning on nonconvex optimization problems, along with the increasing complexity and scale of these problems, the demand for more efficient and scalable algorithms for nonconvex optimization continuously grows.
elements.sent-2411-016: A particular technique in addressing this challenge is the `coordinate descent' (CD) approach.
elements.sent-2411-017: The main idea in CD is to minimize the multivariate objective function by updating one variable (or a block of variables) at every iteration, while keeping the others fixed.
elements.sent-2411-018: This approach often leads to simple subproblems that can be solved more efficiently, particularly for large-scale optimization problems commonly encountered in data science and related fields.
elements.sent-2411-019: DC programming and DCA have been widely used in machine learning applications.
elements.sent-2411-020: Examples include kernel selection \citep{argyriou2006dc , sparse principal component analysis \citep{sriperumbudur2007sparse , discrepancy estimation in domain adaptation \citep{awasthi2024best , training neural networks \citep{awasthi2024dc , and many more.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, to our knowledge, there is currently no true CD variant of DCA with convergence guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our main goal in this paper is to address this gap.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Block Coordinate DC Algorithm (\algo)} \label{alg:BCDC} \begin{algorithmic} {\setstretch{1.15} \STATE { Input:} Starting point $\bm{x}^{1}\subseteq \mathcal{M}$, and total number of itreations $K$. \FOR{ $k=1$ { to} $K$} \STATE Select a subgradient $\bm{v}^k \in \partial h(\bm{x}^k)$ \STATE Choose $i_k$ from $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ uniformly at random \STATE Find $ x_{i_k}^{k+1} \in \argmin_{x_{i_k} \in \mathcal{M}_{i_k}} f(\bm{x}_{i_k} + \bar{\bm{x}}_{i_k}^{k}) + g_{i_k}(x_{i_k})-\langle v^k_{i_k},x_{i_k} \rangle$ \STATE Set $\bm{x}^{k+1} =\bm{x}_{i_k}^{k+1} + \bar{\bm{x}}_{i_k}^{k}$ \ENDFOR \STATE { Output:} $\bm{x}^{k+1}$ } \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-141: For the broad class of natural exponential family distributions, EM is a specific instance of DCA.
elements.sent-2411-142: The natural exponential family of distributions with a parameter $\bm{\theta}\in\Theta$ is characterized by the following probability mass (or density) function: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:nat-expo-dist} P(\bm{x},\bm{y}|{\bm{\theta}}) = \frac{\varphi(\bm{x},\bm{y}) e^{\ip{\bm{\theta}}{T(\bm{x},\bm{y})}}}{\sum_{\bm{x},\bm{y}} \varphi(\bm{x},\bm{y}) e^{\ip{\bm{\theta}}{T(\bm{x},\bm{y})}}},\end{equation} where $\varphi$ is a positive function (base measure) and $T$ denotes the sufficient statistic.
elements.sent-2411-143: It is easy to see that problem \eqref{eqn:ML with a natural exponential family distribution \eqref{eqn:nat-expo-dist can be written as a DC program, since the log-likelihood function can be decomposed as $\mathcal{L \!=\! f \!-\! h$, where the terms are given by \begin{equation}\label{eqn:functions} f(\bm \theta) = N\log\Big(\sum_{\bm{x},\bm{y}} \varphi(\bm{x},\bm{y}) e^{\ip{\bm{\theta}}{T(\bm{x},\bm{y})}} \Big), \quad \text{and} \quad h(\bm \theta) = \sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\log\Big(\sum_{\bm y} \varphi(\bm{x},\bm{y}) e^{\ip{\bm{\theta}}{T(\bm{x},\bm{y})}} \Big).\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-145: Applying DCA to this problem leads to the following update rule: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:em_deriv} \begin{aligned} \bm{\theta}^{k+1} \in \argmin_{ \bm{\theta}} ~~N\log\left(\sum_{\bm{x}, \bm{y}} \varphi(\bm{x},\bm{y})e^{\ip{\bm{\theta}}{T(\bm{x}, \bm{y})}}\right) - \sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}} \frac{\sum_{\bm{y}} \varphi(\bm{x},\bm{y})\ip{\bm{\theta}}{T(\bm{x}, \bm{y})}e^{\ip{\bm{\theta}^k}{T(\bm{x}, \bm{y})}}}{\sum_{\bm{y}} \varphi(\bm{x},\bm{y})e^{\ip{\bm{\theta}^k}{T(\bm{x}, \bm{y})}}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} After algebraic simplifications, this reduces to: \begin{equation}\theta^{k+1} \in \argmin_{\theta} \sum_{x\inX}\sum_{y}\frac{\varphi(x,y)e^{\ip{\theta^k}{T(x, y)}}}{\sum_{y}\varphi(x,y)e^{\ip{\theta}{T(x, y)}}} \times\Bigg[\log\left(\frac{\sum_{x, y} \varphi(x,y)e^{\ip{\theta}{T(x, y)}}}{\varphi(x,y)e^{\ip{\theta}{T(x, y)}}}\right) \Bigg]\end{equation} which is exactly the EM algorithm for the exponential family.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Block EM Algorithm} \label{alg:BCEM} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} total iterations $K$, initialize the probability distribution $P$ using vector $\bm{\theta}^{(0)}$ ($P$ belongs to the exponential family), and number of blocks $n$ \FOR{ $k=1$ { to} $K$} \STATE Randomly choose $i_k$ in $[1,...,n]$ with uniform distribution \STATE Update $ \hat{P}^{k+1}(\bm{y}) =\frac{P(\bm{x},\bm{y}|\bm{\theta}^k)}{\sum_{\bm{y}} P(\bm{x},\bm{y}|\bm{\theta}^k)}$ \STATE Update ${\theta}_{i_k}^{k+1} = \underset{\theta_{i_k}}{\mathrm{arg\,min}} -\sum_{\bm{x}\in\mathcal{X},\bm{y}}\hat{P}^{k+1}(\bm{y})\log P(\bm{x},\bm{y}|\theta^k_{1},\ldots,\theta_{i_k},\ldots,\theta^k_{n}) $ \STATE Set $\bm \theta^{k+1} = \bar{\bm \theta}_{i_k}^{k}+ \bm \theta_{i_k}^{k+1}$ \ENDFOR \STATE { Output: $\bm{\theta}^{K+1}$} \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-157: We investigated a general class of DC problems involving smooth and separable non-smooth terms, and proposed a randomized block-coordinate DC algorithm (\algo) specifically designed to solve such problems efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-158: We established convergence guarantees for \algo with a rate of $\mathcal{O (n/K)$, where the algorithm performs $K$ iteration over $n$ coordinate blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Leveraging the connection between the DCA and the EM method, we proposed a block-coordinate EM algorithm (\algoem), where convergence guarantees are directly inherited from the DCA as a special case.
elements.sent-2411-160: DCA and EM have numerous applications in data science and machine learning; hence, scalable alternatives like \algo and \algoem have the potential for significant impact.
elements.sent-2411-161: As part of our analysis, we identified tractable first-order stationarity conditions for non-smooth non-convex optimization problems based on a DC representation.
elements.sent-2411-162: Given the prevalence of non-smooth non-convex problems in machine learning applications (e.g., ReLU neural networks and other models with non-differentiable activation functions) and the number of open questions surrounding their non-asymptotic convergence analysis, we believe our findings should be of broad interest to the optimization community within machine learning research.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Block REM Algorithm} \label{alg:BCREM} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} regularization parameter $\lambda$, total iterations $K$, initialize the probability distribution $P$ using vector $\bm{\theta}^{(0)}$ ($P$ belongs to the exponential family), and number of blocks $n$ \FOR{ $k=1$ { to} $K$} \STATE Randomly choose $i_k$ in $[1,...,n]$ with uniform distribution \STATE Update $ \hat{P}^{k+1}(\bm{y}) =\frac{P(\bm{x},\bm{y}|\bm{\theta}^k)}{\sum_{\bm{y}} P(\bm{x},\bm{y}|\bm{\theta}^k)}$ \STATE Update ${\theta}_{i_k}^{k+1} = \underset{\theta_{i_k} \in \Theta_{i_k}}{\mathrm{arg\,min}} \sum_{\bm{x}\in\mathcal{X},\bm{y}}-\hat{P}^{k+1}(\bm{y})\log P(\bm{x},\bm{y}|\theta^k_{1},\ldots,\theta_{i_k},\ldots,\theta^k_{n}) + \lambda R_{i_k}(\theta_{i_k})$ \STATE Set $\bm{\theta}^{k+1} = \bar{\bm{\theta}}_{i_k}^{k}+ \bm{\theta}_{i_k}^{k+1}$ \ENDFOR \STATE { Output: $\bm{\theta}^{K+1}$} \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-010: Application to One-bit MIMO Signal Recovery
elements.sent-2411-207: In this section, we will use the proposed Block EM method in one-bit MIMO signal recovery problem.
elements.sent-2411-209: Later, simulation results are given to show the performance of our method compared to the conventional EM method.
elements.sent-2411-210: Consider a MIMO uplink scenario where $N$ transmitters (users) simultaneously send a signal of length $W$ to the Base-Station (BS) which consists of $M$ receiving antennas.
elements.sent-2411-211: The signal will undergo a frequency selective fading channel.
elements.sent-2411-212: Then, the recieved signal at the BS will have the form \begin{equation}\label{MIMO_prob} \boldsymbol R_m = \sum_{n=1}^N \boldsymbol H_{m,n} \boldsymbol \theta_n + \boldsymbol \alpha_m \quad and \quad Y_m = Q(R_m), \quad for \quad m=1,\ldots,M,\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol R_m\in \mathbb C^{W}$ is the received signal at $m$th antenna, $\boldsymbol H_{m,n}\in \mathbb C^{W\times W $ denotes the channel circulant matrix between the $n$the user and $m$th receiver, $\boldsymbol \theta_n\in \mathbb C^W$ is the transmitted signal of the $n$th user, and $\alpha_m\in\mathbb C^W$ is the complex additive white Gaussian noise for the $m$th receiver with mean $\boldsymbol 0$ and covariance matrix $\sigma_{\alpha ^2 \boldsymbol I$.}}
elements.formula-2411-058: \begin{equation}\label{MIMO_prob} \boldsymbol R_m = \sum_{n=1}^N \boldsymbol H_{m,n} \boldsymbol \theta_n + \boldsymbol \alpha_m \quad and \quad Y_m = Q(R_m), \quad for \quad m=1,\ldots,M,\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-283: We set $\lambda = 0.1/m$ and $\lambda=10/m$, and use blocks of size $1000$.
elements.sent-2411-285: In this example, \algo and DCA perform similarly in terms of the number of full passes (iteration $\times$ block-size$/m$) for both the objective and the gap function (as defined in \Cref{def:gap ).}
elements.sent-2411-286: However, \algo avoids solving full-scale subproblems, making it suitable for devices with limited computational power.
elements.sent-2411-287: It also offers greater potential for parallelization through asynchronous updates, which could be a valuable direction for future research.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Nonconvex Quadratic Programming with Negative $\ell_1$ Regularization
elements.sent-2411-288: The second experiment examines nonconvex quadratic programming with a negative $\ell_1$ norm regularization term to promote binary solutions, formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:qubo} \min_{\|\bm x\|_{\infty}\leq 1} ~~ \bm x^T\bm Q \bm x -\lambda \|\bm x\|_1,\end{equation} where $\bm Q\in\mathbb{R ^{m\times m }$ is the symmetric (not necessarily positive semidefinite) cost matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-289: We conduct experiments using the GSet benchmark datasets \cite{Gset , setting the negative adjacency matrix as the cost matrix $\bm Q$.}
elements.sent-2411-291: In this formulation, we first consider the following decomposition: $f(\bm x)= \bm x^T\bm Q \bm x,~g(\bm x)=0,~ h(\bm x)=\lambda \|\bm x\|_1,$ and $\mathcal{M =[-1,1]^m$ is the separable constraint set.}
elements.sent-2411-292: It is important to note that $f(\bm x)$ is nonconvex, but this does not affect the validity of our guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-293: It is easy to verify that our guarantees hold for any smooth $f(\bm x)$ as long as the subproblems can be solved globally.
elements.sent-2411-294: While nonconvex $f(\bm x)$ makes the subproblems nonconvex, solving them globally along a single coordinate is tractable, as demonstrated in this example.
elements.sent-2411-295: For DCA, the subproblem becomes: \begin{equation}\min_{\|\bm x\|_{\infty}\leq 1} ~~ \bm x^T\bm Q \bm x -\lambda \langle \text{sign}(\bm x^k),\bm x - \bm x^k \rangle,\end{equation} which is generally NP-hard.
elements.formula-2411-076: \begin{equation}\min_{\|\bm x\|_{\infty}\leq 1} ~~ \bm x^T\bm Q \bm x -\lambda \langle sign(\bm x^k),\bm x - \bm x^k \rangle,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-296: However, using \algo with block size 1, the subproblem simplifies to \begin{equation}\min_{x_i \in [-1,1]} ~~ (\bar{\bm x}_i^k + \bm x_i)^T\bm Q (\bar{\bm x}_i^k + \bm x_i) -\lambda \langle \text{sign}(\bm x^k),\bar{\bm x}_i^k + \bm x_i - \bm x^k \rangle.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-077: \begin{equation}\min_{x_i \in [-1,1]} ~~ (\bar{\bm x}_i^k + \bm x_i)^T\bm Q (\bar{\bm x}_i^k + \bm x_i) -\lambda \langle sign(\bm x^k),\bar{\bm x}_i^k + \bm x_i - \bm x^k \rangle.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-297: Expanding this expression gives \begin{equation}x_i^{k+1} = \argmin_{x_i \in [-1,1]}~~ ax_i^2 + bx_i + c\end{equation} for scalars $a = q_{ii}$, $b = 2\bm q_{i ^\top\bar{\bm x _i^k - \lambda \text{sign (x_i^k)$, and $c = (\bar{\bm x _i^k)^T \bm Q \bar{\bm x _i^k + \lambda \text{sign (x_i^k)x_i^k$, where $q_{ii}$ denotes the $i$th diagonal entry of $\bm{Q}$ and $\bm{q _i$ is the $i$th column.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-298: This expression can be efficiently solved, highlighting the practical advantages of \algo.
elements.sent-2411-299: Even in scenarios where DCA involves NP-hard subproblems, \algo remains efficient.
elements.sent-2411-300: It is worth noting that DCA could be applied by splitting $\bm Q = \bm Q_P + \bm Q_N$ where $\bm Q_P$ and $\bm Q_N$ represent positive and negative semidefinite components of $\bm Q$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-301: Under this decomposition, one could redefine $f(\bm x) = \bm x^T\bm Q_P\bm x$ and $h(\bm x) = \lambda \|\bm x\|_1-\bm x^T\bm Q_N\bm x$.
elements.sent-2411-302: However, this approach requires eigendecomposition of $\bm Q$, which can be computationally expensive when $m$ is large.
elements.sent-2411-303: Moreover, $\bm Q_P$ and $\bm Q_N$ may lose useful structural properties.
elements.sent-2411-304: For example, when $\bm Q$ is sparse, $\bm Q_P$ and $\bm Q_N$ may be dense.
elements.sent-2411-305: We also compare with the performance of RCSD from \cite{deng2020efficiency .}
elements.sent-2411-306: Notably, RCSD can be viewed as a special case of BDCA applied to the following reformulation of the problem: \begin{equation}\min_{\|\bm x\|_{\infty}\leq 1} ~~ \frac{L}{2}\|\bm x\|_2^2-\lambda \|\bm x\|_1 -(\frac{L}{2}\|\bm x\|_2^2 - \bm x^T\bm Q \bm x),\end{equation} where $L = 2\|\bm Q\|$ is the smoothness constant.
elements.formula-2411-079: \begin{equation}\min_{\|\bm x\|_{\infty}\leq 1} ~~ \frac{L}{2}\|\bm x\|_2^2-\lambda \|\bm x\|_1 -(\frac{L}{2}\|\bm x\|_2^2 - \bm x^T\bm Q \bm x),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-307: We compared DCA, \algo, and RCSD in terms of objective values at the local solutions found by the methods, and the computational time needed to reach such solution.
elements.sent-2411-308: Both algorithms are initialized from the same random starting point where entries are drawn from a Gaussian distribution, and run until the gap reaches $10^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-310: Specifically, in 45 cases, \algo finds a better local optimum than DCA, while DCA performs better only in 5 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-311: Also, in 31 cases \algo outperforms RCSD and for 11 datasets RCSD finds a better solution.
elements.sent-2411-312: In most cases, \algo is faster than both DCA and RCSD.
elements.sent-2411-313: We investigated a general class of DC problems involving smooth and separable nonsmooth terms, and proposed a randomized block-coordinate DC algorithm (\algo) specifically designed to solve such problems efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-314: As part of our analysis, we identified tractable first-order stationarity conditions for nonsmooth nonconvex optimization problems based on a DC representation.
elements.sent-2411-315: Given the prevalence of nonsmooth nonconvex problems in machine learning applications (e.g., ReLU neural networks and other models with non-differentiable activation functions) and the number of open questions surrounding their non-asymptotic convergence analysis, we believe our findings should be of broad interest to the optimization and machine learning communities.
